data.title: NNN REIT: A Dividend Aristocrat In Net Lease With Hidden Upside
data.description:
Thesis

NNN REIT (NNN) seems to combine the best of both worlds: a rare reliability in dividend distribution and, at the same time, growth prospects arising from targeted acquisitions and a strong capital structure.

In my own assessment, the company is currently at a stage where the investor can lock in an attractive dividend yield of around 5.6%, without worrying much about financial instability or increased operational risk.

The secret lies in how management handles the cycle, selling weak properties, recycling capital into new investments with strong tenants and long-term contracts, while strengthening its liquidity through careful debt issuances.

All this creates a solid base for continued AFFO and dividend growth, with potential outperformance compared to the sector.

The stock deserves a buy recommendation, which fits very defensive equity portfolios.

Stock Overview

The stock of NNN has gained steady investor trust thanks to its long-term record of consistency.

From the point of view of stock performance, NNN does not impress with explosive rallies, but rewards steadily through dividend.

The stock is obviously very defensive, something that is not necessarily bad. On the contrary, it fits exactly a likewise defensive investor, who seeks steady, very secured dividends (as we will see also below) from a very high-quality company, without worrying much about the fluctuations in the stock price.

Comparing with other very good companies in the sector, such as Regency Centers (REG), Agree (ADC), Getty Realty (GTY), Realty Income (O) and Brixmor Property Group (BRX), we can see that it stands decently, neither impressing nor disappointing.

In any case, the “moderate” return it offers comes along with the high degree of safety that things will continue smoothly. Something that interests those long-term investors who want to be positioned for years, enjoying a safe dividend.

5.6% Yield With Room To Grow

In my opinion, the strongest card of NNN is the dividend. And it is not only about the 5.6% yield, which is already attractive. What matters is the consistency, 36 consecutive years of increases. In a REIT universe where many struggle to keep distributions stable, NNN manages to build year by year greater trust.

The recent dividend increase by 3.4% (to $0,60 per quarter) signals that management sees the AFFO sufficiently covered. With payout ratio at 71% of AFFO, there is a significant safety margin. For the second quarter, the free cash flow after the distribution was about $50 million, offering room for reinvestment or future increases.

What convinces me more is that the dividend increases are not based on occasional profits, but on a steady growth of AFFO. The guidance for 2025 was raised to $3,40, $3,45, compared to the previous estimate of $3,39, something that shows consistency in the goal of long-term growth of 1% to 2% per year.

High is also the quality of cash flow, even in weaker markets, the reductions were limited, a fact that strengthens the image of a “sleep well at night” REIT.

In the second quarter of 2025, the company announced core FFO $0,84 per share and AFFO $0.85, increased by 1.2% on an annual basis.

The annual base rent [ABR] reached $894 million, increased by almost 7% compared to last year, while the net operating income margin (NOI margin) was at 98%, levels that show excellent operational control, while it presents better alignment of revenues and expenses and higher stability of return.

Recycling Capital Into 17-Year Leases

NNN operates with an unexciting yet productive business model. The company focuses on buying single-tenant properties, which generate 7.4% initial returns and maintain leases for more than 17 years on average.

The company continues its investments in Q2 2025 by buying 45 new properties worth $230 million. Additionally, spent $460 million on total acquisitions during the semester. This shows that the company remains active through strategic partnership deals, which resulted in 8 out of 11 transactions with existing partners, thus minimizing potential deal failures.

The management team actively removes properties from their portfolio when they fail to show promising growth potential. The second quarter brought $50 million from selling 23 properties, while the half-year sales reached $65 million from 33 properties. The company demonstrates effective value creation through capital recycling because it sells properties at prices 170 basis points below its new investment yields.

The lease renewal rate reached 85% with rent increases at 8% above previous levels, and new leases provided 5% rent growth. The retail portfolio demonstrates strong market performance because it withstands the current retail market challenges. The “At Home” bankruptcy serves as an example to prove the rule because NNN owns strategically located properties that attract new tenants when existing leases expire.

$1.5B Liquidity, 11-Year Debt, Zero Surprises

The capital structure of NNN is among the safest in the sector. With average debt maturity of 11 years and no exposure to floating or secured borrowing, the company enjoys rare stability. The $500 million 5-year bond with a coupon of 4.6%, which was issued recently, strengthened liquidity, which reaches $1,5 billion.

In my opinion, the key element is that the company can fully finance this year’s acquisitions ($600-$700 million) without depending on external capital under pressure. Leverage is around 5,7x, slightly increased compared to the first quarter, but expected due to advanced acquisitions. The CFO has set a target of returning below 5,5x, something that I consider realistic, given the strong free cash flow.

In addition, the company took advantage of the rise of the stock to issue 254,000 new shares through ATM, raising $11 million. This selective use of equity shows healthy management of cost of capital and not dependence on continuous dilution.

Risks: Tenant Failures, Tariff Shocks & The Battle For Yield

There are factors that could create challenges. The macroeconomic environment is one factor. Higher interest rates don’t directly hurt the company because of its long-term debt, but they make the competition tougher. This leads to compressed cap rates, especially in large portfolios, reducing the ability of the Company to invest comfortably. I consider that the upcoming decrease of interest rates from the Fed will not create particular problems for the Company. On the contrary there may be buying interest. NNN attracts defensive portfolios, that may want to take slightly higher risk than other kinds of safe investments, such as bonds, for correspondingly slightly higher return. Meaning that these investors will prefer companies, like NNN, when the bond yields will show a decrease.

Then, there is the tenant risk. Although the portfolio is diversified with more than 400 tenants, the recent bankruptcy of "At Home" shows that retail chains are not invulnerable. Even though NNN has good property positioning, a series of bankruptcies could pressure AFFO, even temporarily. The restaurant sector and furniture, consumer goods are more vulnerable to recession or to increased tariffs.

A third element is the portfolio recycling strategy. Although until today it has proved profitable, it depends on the company always finding buyers at good valuations. If liquidity in the sector decreases, NNN may struggle to dispose of weak properties without losses.

Finally, political and tax factors, such as changes in digital taxes or tariff measures, can hurt tenants and indirectly increase risk. The company itself recognizes it, incorporating 60 bps of bad debt in the forecasts for 2025. Although until now only 15% has been used, these risks are not negligible.

The risks are manageable, but investors should be aware that the stock is not fully protected against recession or massive pressure in retail.

Conclusion

In my opinion, NNN is a stock that deserves to be in every portfolio that seeks a combination of steady income and predictable growth.

With dividend yield of 5.6% (record of 36 consecutive increases), strong capital structure, and a careful buy and sell strategy, it offers reassurance in an environment full of uncertainty.

Despite the risks related to tenants and the broader economic framework, management shows that it knows how to protect the value of the portfolio.

With the guidance for AFFO increasing and the balance sheet remaining strong, I consider that the stock maintains upward momentum.

data.title: Nvidia: Data Center Growth Slowdown Is Expected To Continue Into CY2026
data.description:
Buyside Consensus Was Higher

NVIDIA Corporation's (NASDAQ:NVDA) (TSX:NVDA:CA) has continued to rally since its April low and has become one of the most important market indicators. Why? Because the equity market is largely driven by AI-related growth, which has pushed valuations higher. NVDA remains the best-in-class AI play. I believe that we are in a totally different market cycle. The GenAI boom is unique compared to previous economic cycles. AI growth momentum can significantly lift market sentiment and overshadow S&P 500 names outside of the Magnificent 7. The stronger the AI-related growth, the higher the market consensus, but the key question is: when will demand eventually slow?

The stock got hit with an initial 4% pullback following 2Q FY2026 earnings, but those losses were mostly recouped the next trading day. 2Q Data Center revenue missed market expectations, and management signaled that H20 hasn't contributed to revenue in China.

In my last coverage, I downgraded the stock from Buy to Hold, citing the risk of the stock rising too far, too fast. However, a neutral rating doesn't mean the stock has no long-term value. It means that chasing the rally at the current levels could face higher downside risks. Timing the market is difficult, and investors seem increasingly comfortable with high valuations supported by strong AI demand. Given the ongoing growth deceleration, I believe upside earnings surprises could narrow. Moreover, signs of an AI demand slowdown in 2026 may create cyclical headwinds for NVDA. Therefore, I'm cautious on the recent rally and reiterating a Hold rating on the stock.

2Q FY2026 Earnings Takeaway

NVDA delivered strong 2Q FY2026 earnings, beating both revenue and non-GAAP EPS consensus, driven by strong demand for Blackwell chips (GB200 and GB300). However, growth expectations were already priced in, particularly around Data Center revenue, which grew 56.4% YoY growth but fell short of buyside consensus. As shown on the chart, growth momentum still remains impressive but is starting to normalize.

Despite H20 headwinds in 2Q, the company still achieved a 5% sequential growth in Data Center. During the earnings call, Jensen Hwang noted that H20 revenue declined by $4 billion due to missed China sales under licensing restrictions. However, NVDA released nearly $180 million of previously reserved H20 inventory, helping lift the growth margin to 72.7%.

While the U.S. has lifted the H20 ban for some Chinese customers and Jensen even said that "bringing Blackwell to China is a real possibility", I think uncertainties remain high. The PBOC recently expressed concerns over information risks and urged Chinese firms to avoid H20 chips. Even if they adopt them, competition from domestic AI alternatives such as Huawei is intensifying. On top of that, the U.S. government has also signaled that they want a 15% cut of H20 revenue from China sales. That said, political risk for NVDA in China remains high, at least for now.

3Q FY2026 Guidance: Growth Slowdown Continues, Margins Improving

Looking ahead, its 3Q FY2026 guidance signals that continued strong AI demand. The midpoint of 3Q revenue guidance implies 53.9% YoY growth, showing a sequential slowdown. However, management noted that this outlook excludes H20 revenue from China. If licensing issues resolve, the company expects an additional $2 billion to $5 billion in revenue, which could lift to 64% YoY (assuming $3.5 billion H20 revenue from China), indicating a sequential rebound.

Gross margin is expected to recover to 73.5% in 3Q. Management had previously guided for gross margins in the mid-70s by late FY2026, suggesting 4Q FY2026 gross margin could increase another 100-150 bps QoQ. Over the longer term, Jensen reiterated a $3-4 trillion AI infrastructure TAM by 2030. Overall, management sees near-term demand remains extraordinarily strong, but growth momentum is gradually trending lower, as shown on the chart.

Potential Slowdown in AI Spending in CY2026?

The stock's impact on whether AI spending continues to be robust or softens will depend on how much is already priced in. According to a recent report, overall data center spend is expected to grow 42% YoY in 2025, accelerating from 40.3% YoY in 2024. This has fueled robust ongoing AI demand. However, we saw NVDA's revenue growth is slowing due to the law of large numbers.

Gartner projects a sharp slowdown in 2026, with data center spending growth dropping to 16.3%. Meanwhile, despite some de-escalation in tariffs, Trump's trade war is not over. If tariff risk increases, the uncertainty could lead companies to pause new AI projects, as the report indicated.

Recently, Trump has threatened to impose new tariffs and export controls on advanced technology and semiconductors in response to digital services taxes and has also floated a potential 200% tariff on China over a rare-earth dispute. It seems the market has shrugged off these risks and decided not to react until they actually materialize.

The key question is whether AI demand can once again surpass market expectations and whether the next-gen AI platform, Rubin, can sustain growth momentum. Rubin is scheduled for volume production in 2026.

Can Valuation Expand Further?

The short answer is yes. But my neutral view reflects the fact that growth is unlikely to be linear. NVDA has rallied more than 12 folds in 5 years, reflecting its best-in-class growth profile but also banking on lofty AI expectations. Despite long-term secular AI tailwinds, I believe the data center business will eventually revert to a more cyclical pattern, which can cause a stock pullback.

At 41x non-GAAP P/E, NVDA doesn't look too expensive given the high valuation of equity market. However, regulatory headwinds and intensifying Chinese competition could reshape long-term growth expectations. Since the relentless rally implies that the company must deliver consistently extraordinary results going forward, I believe the risk of missing expectations is higher, particularly as it will need to explore new market opportunities to sustain long-term growth. However, I think it's fine to hold existing positions, but it seems imprudent to chase the rally at current levels.

Conclusion

In the near term, NVDA's stock momentum could continue, as its 2Q FY2026 earnings demonstrated resiliency and management signaled no slowdown in AI demand. In the mid to long term, however, the miss in Data Center revenue and slowing growth, political risks, and potential demand for H20 chips should be cautious. The stock's valuation has nearly priced in perfection, which is reasonable given the current AI demand. After a straight-line rally, the risk of the near-term consolidation is higher, and the upside potential is limited. I see more attractive buying opportunities around the $150 level and remain Hold on NVDA for now.

data.title: CrowdStrike: Valuation Potential Fully Realized
data.description:
Cybersecurity firm CrowdStrike (CRWD) reported better-than-expected earnings for its second fiscal quarter on Wednesday due to strong growth in its customer pool and expansion in its annual recurring revenue base. However, CrowdStrike’s guidance for the third quarter disappointed investors, leading to a 4% drop of the company’s shares in the after-hours market.

CrowdStrike remained widely profitable on a free cash flow basis and continues to grow its ARR business at 20% annual growth rates, with an acceleration expected in the second half of the year. While the ARR performance was very strong, and the outlook for Q3 was not bad, I believe CrowdStrike has very limited upside revaluation potential. In my opinion, the cybersecurity platform's ARR and earnings growth potential is more than fully reflected in the company's valuation, and I maintain a rating of sell at the current valuation point.

Previous rating

I downgraded shares of CrowdStrike in May 2025 due to concerns about valuation: Time To Take Profits. Since then shares have dropped about 8%, not including the 4% after-hours drop on Wednesday. I like that CrowdStrike is making gains in terms of its ARR business and that the cybersecurity platform is heavily involved in growing its AI offers. However, given the stretched valuation based off of revenue, I believe the upside potential is likely limited here.

CrowdStrike beat Q2 '26 estimates

The cybersecurity enterprise reported better-than-expected results on both the top and bottom lines for the second fiscal quarter: CrowdStrike generated $0.93 in adjusted earnings per share in the June quarter, which beat the consensus estimate by $0.10 per share. Revenues came in at $1.17B, beating the average prediction by $20M.

The second quarter was a good one for CrowdStrike, which benefited from continual customer acquisition in the enterprise market and increasing annual recurring revenues. The cybersecurity platform generated $1,169M in revenue in Q2 '26, showing 21% year-over-year growth. The important metric of 'subscription revenue' came in at $1,103M, also showing 20% year-over-year growth. Subscription revenue, which forms the basis for CrowdStrike's growing ARR, represented 95% of the platform's total revenue in the June quarter.

What stood out from CrowdStrike’s Q2 '26 earnings scorecard was that the company continued to make considerable progress in building its ARR business. ARR stands for annual recurring revenue and is the result of subscription solutions for which customers pay on a recurring basis.

CrowdStrike's ARR stood at $4,657M at the end of Q2 '26, meaning the cybersecurity enterprise added $792M in annual recurring revenue in the trailing-twelve-month period, which calculates to about $200M per quarter. The company's ARR is growing at 20% annually, but the firm has guided for at least 40% net new ARR growth in the second half of the year as enterprise customers adopt the firm's Cloud and AI-driven products and services.

At the current rate of growth, the cybersecurity platform is on track to reach at least $5.0B in ARR by the end of the current fiscal year. A sustained 20% expansion rate in annual recurring revenue in the next five years could put the company on track to $10.0B in ARR by FY 2030.

In terms of free cash flow, CrowdStrike also had a decent second fiscal quarter: it generated $284M in FCF, showing 4% year-over-year growth. The free cash flow margin was 24%, down 4 PP Y/Y. While the free cash flow margin dropped year-over-year, CrowdStrike has been consistently profitable in the last twelve months and should remain so going forward.

Outlook Q3 '26 disappointed

CrowdStrike guided for $1,208-1,218M in revenue for the third quarter, which was below the consensus estimate of $1,230M. While the guidance for Q3 '26 fell short of the estimate, the company raised its full-year top line outlook at the low-end by $6M (to a new guidance low point of $4,749.5M) while its EPS outlook was raised from $3.44-3.56/share to $3.60-3.72/share. The slightly disappointing Q3 '26 outlook likely explains why shares of the cybersecurity platform dropped 4% in the after-hours market.

CrowdStrike’s earnings growth potential is likely overvalued

In my last work on the cybersecurity platform, I stated that I was concerned about the company’s high valuation based off of forward revenue, which is why I downgraded CrowdStrike to sell at the time. Shares of CrowdStrike are currently valued at a FY 2026 revenue multiplier of 18.0X, which implies that the cybersecurity business is trading approximately 30% above its 3-year average price-to-revenue ratio of 13.9X. The high valuation relative to the historical average is an indication that CrowdStrike is still overvalued: if shares of CrowdStrike were to revalue to the historical P/S average of 13.9X, they could have a fair value closer to $326/share, implying about 23% downside correction potential.

Other cloud-based cybersecurity platforms that offer Cloud, Network and Endpoint security include Palo Alto (PANW), SentinelOne (S) and Zscaler (ZS) which I am using in my industry group below for comparison purposes. CrowdStrike is still the most expensive cybersecurity play here for investors and is currently priced at a 63% premium to the industry group average price-to-revenue ratio of only 11.1X.

Risks with CrowdStrike

The biggest risk for CrowdStrike is a slowdown in the ARR business, which is essential to backing up the cybersecurity company’s valuation. Strong ARR growth (20%+ annually), however, seems to me to be already priced into the firm's valuation, so a slowdown in annual recurring revenue expansion is likely the biggest risk factor for the company right now. What would get me to change my mind about the platform is the use of FCF for new revenue-boosting acquisitions or an acceleration in the firm's ARR growth trajectory.

Final thoughts

Although CrowdStrike submitted a very solid earnings sheet for the second quarter with 20%+ Y/Y growth in revenue, strong FCF, and a double beat on earnings and revenue, I stand by my previous conclusion that the cybersecurity platform is likely overvalued, based off of revenue, and therefore has an unattractive risk profile for investors. I see long-term ARR growth potential for the platform in an environment where the cyberthreat landscape is constantly evolving. However, I believe that CrowdStrike's long-term ARR and revenue growth potential in the cybersecurity market is more than fully reflected in the company’s valuation at this point... which is why I confirm my sell rating for CrowdStrike following the Q2 '26 earnings report.

data.title: Risk Managing An Evergreen Private Credit Portfolio
data.description:
By Mark Garfinkel

As the private credit market surges toward $3 trillion in assets1, concerns are growing over the potential formation of a bubble—driven by too much capital chasing too few high-profile lending opportunities. Rather than focusing on whether the market is in a bubble, this article explores how to strategically manage risk within a private credit portfolio.

Key Highlights:

  • Capital Concentration in Large Loans: Most capital inflows have targeted larger, private equity-sponsored businesses with EBITDA over $100M. This space is now highly efficient and saturated, characterized by tighter spreads, commoditized loans, and weakening loan terms—challenging risk-adjusted return potential.
  • Potential Alpha in Inefficient Markets: Risk can be better managed by investing in less competitive, undercapitalized niches such as smaller borrowers, specialty lending, and asset-based finance. These areas can offer higher yields, better loan terms, and lower loan-to-value ratios due to reduced competition.
  • Three Pillars of Risk Management:
    1. Diversification across segments, strategies, and issuers to mitigate volatility and avoid concentration risk.
    2. Credit Management through stringent underwriting, partner selection, and disciplined loan structuring.
    3. Liquidity Management using short-duration, laddered investments and cash-equivalent buffers to preserve flexibility and access.
  • Not All Private Credit Is Created Equal: Like equities, the private credit market is heterogeneous. Opportunities vary dramatically by market segment, and risk-adjusted return potential is highest in those niches that are overlooked and underfunded.

Ultimately, prudent portfolio construction and active risk management—especially outside of crowded upper-market segments—are the keys to the opportunity for enhanced risk-adjusted returns from private credit investing in 2025 and beyond.

Risk Managing a Private Credit Portfolio in a Crowded Market

As private credit grows into a $3 trillion asset class, concerns over risk and overvaluation loom large. Yet, the solution isn’t avoiding private credit—it’s about managing it wisely.

Private credit refers to loans made by non-bank lenders directly to private businesses, a segment that expanded rapidly post-2008 as regulatory constraints limited bank lending. Today, the market faces a new challenge: excess capital flowing into a narrow segment of large, private equity-sponsored companies.

Private Credit Explosion Across Debt Types

This chart illustrating how combined credit categories—including distressed, venture, real estate, BDCs, and infrastructure debt—have surged more than tenfold post‑GFC.

Where the Capital is Going

Most inflows have chased large borrowers—companies with EBITDA above $100 million and loan needs well into the hundreds of millions. This influx has commoditized that part of the market. With widespread competition, interest spreads have compressed, covenants have loosened, and lenders have taken on more risk to win deals.

This chart demonstrates that most private credit deal flow continues to favor larger borrowers (>$100M), with the highest capital concentration in the mid to upper end of the market. Notable takeaways:

  • Borrowers under $100M remain underrepresented in terms of volume, even as total market deal flow grows.
  • From 2015 to 2023, deal volume peaked around 2022, with notable share growth in $1B+ deals

In this highly efficient environment, outsized returns become harder to find. But does that mean all private credit is overvalued? Not at all.

Value Flows from the Forgotten Streams

The largest areas of the private lending market, much like the largest river systems, have been fully explored and mapped. These are the obvious, well-traveled channels of capital that are visible, deep, and widely pursued by institutional investors because it is familiar, liquid, and scalable. Yet there are thousands, if not millions, of smaller tributaries ripe for further exploration. While these areas are not always as obvious to the naked eye, proper exploration can identify undercapitalized and/or misunderstood lending niches that flow with potential. The overlooked corners of the private credit world—small businesses, niche lenders, and specialized asset-based finance—offer higher yields, tighter covenants, and better collateralization. These borrowers are often too small for large private credit firms and constrained by traditional bank lending standards.

This conceptual graphic is designed to illustrate:

  • Heavy capital chasing large PE-sponsored loans (EBITDA > $100M, commoditized, covenant-light)
  • Smaller, underserved lending niches with stronger protections and yield

For risk-conscious investors, this is fertile ground. Markets with fewer lenders and less capital create favorable dynamics: lenders can command better terms, reduce loan-to-value, and maintain underwriting discipline. These inefficiencies enable enhanced risk-adjusted returns without taking undue risk.

Risk Management Framework

The most effective way to manage risk in private credit is through a three-pronged strategy:

  1. Diversification
    • Invest across multiple segments (e.g., real estate, legal finance, factoring, consumer finance).
    • Maintain limits on exposure per strategy or borrower.
    • Adjust position sizing to reduce downside risk in the event of a loss in any one segment.
  2. Credit Management
    • Partner with lending specialists who focus on niche markets and have proven underwriting capabilities.
    • Look for strong collateral structures, tight loan terms, and stringent covenant enforcement.
    • Emphasize capital preservation over yield chasing.
  3. Liquidity and Duration Management
    • Incorporate short-term private investments and liquid public assets to maintain flexibility.
    • Ladder maturities to provide periodic reinvestment opportunities.
    • Keep overall portfolio duration short to adapt quickly to changing market conditions.

Private Credit is not Monolithic

The phrase "private credit" encompasses a vast and varied universe. Treating it as a single asset class ignores the crucial differences across market segments. Just as one wouldn’t evaluate all equities through the lens of tech stocks, one shouldn’t judge private credit solely by the dynamics of large direct lending deals.

There’s opportunity in specialization—and risk in generalization.

Final Thought

Private credit remains a compelling tool for generating income and diversification. However, sustainable success requires strategic allocation, focused underwriting, and active management. In a market increasingly dominated by large players chasing the same deals, the real potential alpha lies off the beaten path.

In private credit, it pays to be selective. The optimal fishing holes are often the quietest ones.

Sourcing

  • Private credit spans diverse sectors Heron Finance+15NAIC+15Citco+15Citco+1Calamos Investmentscommeketa.com+1Apollo+7PwC+7flow.db.com+7S&P Global.
  • Direct lending is only part of the picture—specialty and niche strategies are significant NAICcom.
  • Private credit has delivered relatively strong performance, particularly in volatile or rising-rate environments Heron Finance.
  • There’s a divergence between crowded upper-market lending and less-saturated opportunities com.Five Charts That Explain Private Credit - Alts.coMarketWatchwsj.com.
  • ¹Global AUM Growth: The asset class is estimated at $1.7 tn by late 2023 and forecast to exceed $3.5 tn by 2028. CFA Institute Daily Browse+5com+5Heron Finance+5
  • ¹2028 AUM Milestone: Moody’s predicts global private credit AUM will hit $3 tn by 2028, driven by factors like lower interest rates, declining defaults, especially in the U.S. and Europe. Moody's
  • Bank Relationships:
    • Large U.S. banks’ lending commitments to private credit vehicles (BDCs, PD funds) surged from ~$8 bn in 2013 Q1 to ~$95 bn by 2024 Q4—a ~19.5% annualized growth. gov+2bostonfed.org+2
    • As of 2023, bank commitments to PE/PC fund sponsors reached ~$300 bn, or approximately 14% of their non‑bank financial institution lending—up from around 1% in 2013. org

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Netflix: Shares Are Getting Perilously Close To Warranting A Downgrade
data.description:
One business that continues to surprise me is streaming giant Netflix (NFLX). Back in April of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘hold’ candidate. After management reported financial results for the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year that exceeded analysts’ expectations, I called it a ‘great show’ that was happening. Growth was continuing and the future looked bright. I don't mind paying a high price for a high-quality company, especially if it operates as an industry leader. But because of how expensive the stock was, I had no choice but to reaffirm it as a ‘hold’ candidate at that time.

Since then, the company has performed pretty much in line with what I anticipated. You see, whenever I rate a company a ‘hold’, I am making the claim that shares are likely to perform pretty much along the lines of the broader market for the foreseeable future. Since that article was published, the stock is up 24.5%. That comes on the back of an upward revision in revenue and profits for this year. However, it also almost perfectly matches the 24% increase enjoyed by the broader market. From a valuation standpoint, I must say that things are getting a bit extreme. I'm not ready to downgrade the company just yet. But it really wouldn't take much more for that to be justified. For now, however, I will keep it as a very cautious 'hold.'

A growth machine

The most recent data that we have regarding Netflix covers through the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. For that time, revenue for the company came in at $11.08 billion. That represents an increase of 15.9% compared to the $9.56 billion the company reported one year earlier. In the past, management used to provide region by region subscriber numbers and monthly ARPU (average revenue per user). To this day, I still think it was a disservice to investors for management to discontinue that. According to management, this move was the result of the company continuing to expand its business model, including in advertising in ways that made a reliance on user-specific data less relevant. But I digress.

According to management, this increase in sales came as a result of more members spread across all regions, higher subscription pricing, and continued growth in advertising revenue. In some areas, like its UCAN (US and Canada) region, revenue growth was 15% year over year.

That in particular was thanks to price changes that the company implemented previously. The business continues to make great progress when it comes to monetizing its world-class platform. As an example, the firm expects to roughly double its advertising revenue this year compared to last year. This follows the rollout of its Netflix Ads Suite, which is its own in-house first-party ad technology platform. The second quarter of 2025 was the first quarter in which this operation had been available to all of its ad markets. Management even described their ad tech platform as being ‘foundational’ to its long-term ads strategy.

On top of this, the business was almost complete at the time that it announced results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, with closing most of its deals with major ad agencies that will allow it to approximately double ads revenue in the US alone for this year.

The company continues to benefit from other things as well. As an example, during the second quarter of this year, management redesigned its TV homepage in a way that they believe will improve member experience. Supposedly, early traction on that has been encouraging. In fact, management even said that it has exceeded expectations.

Of course, no conversation can be had without talking about the core operations of the business. This includes strong performance associated with much of the content on its platform. As an example, during the quarter, Sirens experienced 56 million views. Ginny & Georgia S3 raked in 53 million. New series that are out in the US, such as The Four Seasons and Ransom Canyon, brought in 40 million views and 30 million views, respectively. And some films that the company released did incredibly well.

As an example of this, Extraterritorial which is from Germany, became its fourth most popular non-English language film in the firm's history with 89 million views. But even that does not match the 109 million views that Tyler Perry’s STRAW received. But perhaps the most impressive showing ended up being attributed to Squid Game S3, with a whopping 122 million views. Naturally, management is continuing to focus on upcoming releases as well. Even though a date has not yet been set, the one that I am the most excited for is season 2 of the One Piece live action series. That should come out sometime next year.

Speaking of One Piece, one thing that I need to bring up from a previous article about a different company is the firms position in the anime industry. On August 14th of this year, I published an article about how Webtoon Entertainment Inc. (WBTN) and The Walt Disney Company (DIS) had reached an agreement to partner up in order to bring Disney content, including original Disney content, to its platform. In that article, I pointed out how Netflix had only recently said that half of its global users now watch anime, with anime viewership tripling on the platform over the last five years. The Japanese anime industry is worth around $23.2 billion. And if everything goes according to plan, this industry will see significant growth between now and the end of this decade. As one of the big players in the anime industry, the other one being Crunchyroll, which is owned by Sony (SONY), I fully expect continued growth in the years to come.

The growth that the company saw on its top line allowed bottom line results to improve as well. Net income jumped from $2.15 billion to $3.13 billion year over year. Operating cash flow expanded from $1.29 billion to $2.42 billion. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an approximate doubling from $1.54 billion to $3.11 billion. Finally, EBITDA for the streaming giant grew from $2.68 billion to $3.85 billion. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, are all growing materially year over year.

Things are going so well for Netflix right now that management even decided to increase guidance for this year. They now expect revenue to be between $44.8 billion and $45.2 billion. That compares to the prior expected range of between $43.5 billion and $44.5 billion. For context, last year, the company generated sales of just $39 billion. So if management hits the midpoint of guidance this year, that would be a 15.4% rise in overall sales. On top of this, management expects a small increase in operating margin. Instead of the 29% margin that they expect to previously, they now expect that to be 29.5%. I know this might not sound like much of a difference. But when applied to $45 billion in revenue, that's an extra $225 million in operating income compared to what the picture would be without the upward revision.

If we project out results based on what we have seen so far for the year, we would anticipate net profits of $11.70 billion, adjusted operating cash flow of $12.15 billion, and EBITDA of $14.48 billion. If we use these estimates, and we also use the historical results that Netflix achieved last year, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. Honestly, these are really high multiples. They would normally be high enough for me to easily rate the company a ‘sell’.

But at the same time, this is a quality company that's growing rather rapidly. The firm has only a small amount of debt, $6.06 billion, when stacked up against a market capitalization of $519.90 billion. And we need to consider the stickiness that the platform has experienced in recent years, it's difficult to be bearish.

Takeaway

Right now, I do think that Netflix is a fascinating business. But honestly, it's too expensive for my liking. It's not so expensive that I would downgrade the company just yet. But it is getting there. For now, however, I will be content with maintaining a ‘hold’ rating on the stock.

data.title: BlackRock Large Cap Focus Growth VI Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 06/30/25

  • The fund posted a return of 20.58% (Class I shares) for the second quarter of 2025.
  • The largest contributors to relative performance were investment decisions in the information technology (IT), communication services, and consumer staples sectors. The largest detractors were investment decisions in the real estate, utilities, and financials sectors.
  • The largest exposures were in the IT, communication services, and consumer discretionary sectors. During the quarter, the fund increased its allocations to the IT and financials sectors, and reduced its exposures to the health care and real estate sectors.
Contributors Detractors

Stock selection in the IT sector contributed the most, namely investment decisions in the semiconductors & semiconductor equipment industry. In communication services, allocation decisions in the entertainment industry proved beneficial. Lastly, allocation decisions in the beverages industry in the consumer staples sector boosted relative results.

 The largest detractor was security selection in the real estate sector, notably investment decisions in the real estate management & development industry. In utilities, allocation decisions in the independent power and renewable electricity producers industry were detrimental. Security selection in the financials sector, namely in the capital markets industry, was another meaningful detractor.

Further insight

At quarter-end, markets remain volatile amid trade and geopolitical tensions. However, artificial intelligence (AI) optimism persists, with companies highlighting the technology in earnings calls and strong results. Economic signals are mixed, but we remain grounded in a bottom-up, fundamentals-driven approach. We view this environment as fertile for active management, emphasizing selectivity and discipline. Our focus remains on quality growth companies with durable advantages and underappreciated growth potential. Lower rates in the second half of this year may support expansion, and broadening equity returns beyond the so-called Magnificent Seven large-cap technology companies could benefit niche AI players. We continue to seek differentiated growth opportunities across sectors.

Portfolio management

Phil Ruvinsky, Caroline Bottinelli

data.title: Apple: Building A Bull Case Is As Easy As A-B-Gen Z!
data.description:
I would like to begin my first article on Apple, Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) by saying that Apple is a sure bet. However, having consumed dozens of Apple articles lately and much of the news flows around a potential Alphabet (GOOG) partnership for using Gemini on iPhone, I see the media - if not the market - miss something very critical for future growth: there is a generational market awaiting future iPhones, and this forms the crux of my bullish thesis for AAPL.

Thesis Outline

I shall make some very broad observations as I build my bull case:

  1. An iPhone demand baseline does exist and is evident in 1Q sales trends, and this has several implications that I will attempt to unpack
  2. The install base is vital to future growth of devices as well as services
  3. A demand pull exists far into the future as a generational demand wave

The third point is where the real reason for my bullish views becomes apparent, but it is necessarily to navigate through the first two points to fully appreciate the implications of the third. Join me?

Observation 1: An 'iPhone Demand Baseline' Exists

One can hardly deny that Apple has a largely stable existing user base that will acquire the latest iPhone no matter what. This is not the same as their 'install base', which is the actual number of active iPhones at any given point in time and a much, much larger figure exceeding a billion units. No, this is their baseline sell-through that is almost guaranteed to hit the top line year after year. I shall revisit the install base as we get farther along my train of thought, but for now, one should appreciate the foundational support offered by 'must-upgrade-every-year-iPhone-fanatics', for lack of a better moniker.

This demand type is unique in that it is primarily front-loaded, by which I mean the bulk of new iPhone sales for this cohort happens in the quarter of the device being released. For Apple, this is 1Q, the first fiscal quarter; the holiday quarter that runs from September 29 until December 28 every year.

1Q is essentially the peak quarter for iPhone sales, and this trend began way back in 1Q 2012 and has never broken stride since then. The scheduling of iPhone releases in September is fairly set in stone today because that is what the market expects every year, and to upset this particular cohort with a delayed release would mean turbulence in unit sales and revenues.

Over the years, there have been of lower peaks, just as there were in more recent years, but Apple also has the kind of pricing power that allowed it to breach the $1,000 barrier with their phones in the first place.

I emphasize this because sales revenues are not corroborated by unit volumes despite the fact that the peaks obviously coincide. In this graph below, one sees an interesting but unsurprising pattern revealed.

Please observe how Apple has almost never allowed iPhone unit sales declines to 'interfere' with revenue growth. Apple bulls would love to show off this graph because it lets the power of the brand speak for itself, but it also carries some serious forward implications.

This is where I shall pivot the plot and give it an unpleasant twist. Please note that my intent is to explore the risks to future growth, not to disparage Apple's pricing strategy underpinning what we have just seen in the graph above.

What I now call on investors to focus on is that this could swell into a real problem for Apple if the market stops absorbing their price increases. Even if Apple were able to keep its margins intact in the face of multiple headwinds such as the recently committed $600 billion in investments in the U.S., disruptions to its supply chain from shifting supplier bases, new CapEx arising from these developments, and so on and so forth, the current baseline cohort we identified cannot contribute to growth. All it can do is stay stable and add a modicum of new members to the cohort every year.

Therefore, any iPhone sales growth that Apple demonstrates is over and on top of what the baseline cohort generates. Agreed? We have just established this.

All of a sudden, this becomes a key consideration because, in the graph above, we see that unit sales have generally been flattish to slightly sloping upward. Revenues have grown much faster due to pricing power, a higher number of top-end variants being sold each year, and other related factors but unit sales have not kept up.

So now, I posit that any future growth would necessarily depend on two things: a) marketing aggressiveness, and b) demand pull. This is not to say that brand power is not a driver of future growth, but the real power Apple has over iPhone die-hards is limited from a growth point of view. An arguable point, most definitely, but have we not just seen that the baseline cohort is slow-growing and subject to churn at the other end?

Addressing the first of these future growth drivers, Apple's marketing spend has grown very modestly in the last three full fiscals and still makes up only a portion of 7 percent of topline revenues because it also includes other components of SG&A. It is virtually a sliver of overall revenue and generally intended to support new product launches. With the kind of brand power Apple wields, it is not very surprising that ad spend is this low.

What's left in our growth formula, therefore, is only demand pull. The reason is that the 'iPhone churn' I alluded to above - or Apple losing users to Android devices - is a very real concern. It is a material risk and adds further credence to my inference that additions to the baseline cohort iPhone users are happening at a very slow pace, net of churn.

This is where we arrive at the second observation I initially outlined: that the install base is key to future devices and services growth.

Observation 2: The Install Base of All 'iDevices' Supports Future Growth

The basic foundation has already been set by Apple, and that is its install base or active device users. We saw the iPhone install base crossing a billion units this year, but total device user base across its product lines is now at 2.35 billion (from the same source linked to in that section).

In February 2024, a year before, the install base was at 2.2 million, which calculates to 150 million net new devices added in one year.

I will say "OUCH!" now and explain it.

We are now looking at total device sales of 411 million units in 2024 (adding up the totals across all products from here) but a net addition to the install base of only 150 million.

So the leakage in the install base is massive (and I rarely use that word), and this is precisely why the current install base is critical to future growth.

This leads me to something nearly every analyst on Seeking Alpha has discussed so I will not delve too deep, but a key opportunity for Apple lies in effectively monetizing this hard-to-add-to install base through their ecosystem of services. The Services segment has long been touted as one of the supporting pillars of Apple's revenue and profitability growth, and the behemoth that is the install base is the perfect captive target market for Apple.

This cannot be overstated because, now that we are aware of the high drop-off rate for active devices, we see Services as being an arterial conduit for Apple's revenue growth. More importantly, we also see that as long as the install base in growing, even if only slowly, revenues will keep rising and becoming increasingly less dependent on the iPhone.

Ironically, however, only the growth of iPhone sales can ensure the growth of the install base because Apple's smartphone line-up is the only product line selling in the hundreds of millions. Remember that annual churn was 261 million devices (411 million in sales, less 150 million in install base growth), so you would need a significant offset that can only happen when iPhone sales growth is strong.

Do you see why it is imperative that iPhone sales remain Apple's focus? This also escalates risk to a significant degree, which I shall discuss in the final section.

And that brings me to my third observation.

Observation 3: There is a Measurable Future Demand Pull

We have now seen how the baseline cohort supports iPhone sales, which in turn supports the install base that acts as a substrate for the Services palette. To Apple bears, I say this is not a house of cards that a wisp of headwinds but a painstakingly constructed and dynamic device superstructure that has the strength of stability as well as the power to grow much further, albeit at a gentle pace. One should expect that at this scale.

The real question now is where the future demand is going to come from, and the answer, in one simple term, is "Gen Z".

In a survey of U.S. consumers a couple of years ago, Bloomberg published a very interesting nugget of information:

Bloomberg Intelligence’s 2023 Gen Z Smartphone Survey finds that Apple’s install base may increase by 7% per year, driven by Gen Z adoption, which could push revenue growth to 10% given modest price increases.

I find this highly relevant to my thesis despite the data being two years old or more, the sample size being limited to 1,000 respondents, and the findings being applicable to just the U.S. market.

I extrapolate its relevance to the present day and across the globe because Gen Z cohorts around the world are intricately interconnected due to the Internet and social media. In the words of McKinsey & Company, these are "‘digital natives’ - the first generation to grow up with the internet."

More importantly, the oldest of the Gen Z have been entering the workforce over the past few years, making their own decisions and managing their own finances. That is precisely why that survey data is more important than ever. The Gen Z cohort has not completely come of age yet, but it will over the next ten to twenty years.

What I found even more intriguing is that the Bloomberg survey revealed that "Apple is the clear preference for users between the ages of 18 and 24" and that "this large preference of 79%, compared to the current market share of 41%, may indicate that iOS could gain even more dominance in the region as these consumers age."

That is a significant growth opportunity for Apple because the "18 to 24" age group is just beginning its consumer journey. That creates an extremely long tail for iPhones, and I suspect that sentiment also extends to other iDevices.

In summation, I believe Apple is in an extremely beneficial position to take advantage of this emerging consumer trend, and this is going to support future growth of not only iPhone revenues and unit volumes but also the Services segment that benefits directly from install base growth.

Ending on Risks

And then there is the matter of risk. The biggest risk is an emergent technology that supersedes smartphones and renders the entire market obsolete. RIP the old Nokia. RIP Blackberry. Both of these are mere shadows of what they could have become if Apple had not introduced the first iPhone.

That could very well happen to Apple when the time comes, and that is why it is vital for Apple to push its AI agenda forward even if it means partnering with the likes of Alphabet. It is already an ecosystem expert, so when a new technology comes along that is a potential threat to Apple, the best move would be to quickly acquire it and internalize it. I see this as one of very few ways in which Apple can avoid a seismic disruption to its flagship product.

Another, more organic way would be to innovate on its own and design future-friendly devices, and I do not think Apple is doing that very well. For now, it is clearly protecting its iPhone business, but I am sure investors would like to see more vigorous movement on the innovation side of things.

Another risk is overpaying for the stock. At the current valuation, that does not seem to be a big consideration because it currently trades well below five-year highs on two key valuation ratios; far from cheap in comparison to its sector's median multiples, but Apple deserves whatever premium the market has priced in.

I think the biggest risk now is that if my growth thesis does not pan out, Apple's revenue growth will eventually stagnate. After that, it would be akin to waiting for the business to deteriorate from there and your invested capital, capital gains, and dividend payouts to keep eroding until there is only negative value left.

Very unlikely possibility, in my opinion, because even if you think of the worst-case scenario playing out, Apple has enough cash to buy out practically any emergent technology - and the management and technical expertise that would come with it.

With that, I rate Apple a Buy. Not a Strong Buy because we have not yet entered that renewed growth phase, but I believe it is only a matter of time.

data.title: IM August 2025 - Strong Gold, Specialty Financings Push Totals To 4-Year High
data.description:
Funds raised by junior and intermediate companies increased to a new multiyear high of $2.42 billion in July, marking the highest level since the record high of $3.69 billion in March 2021. This represents a 28% increase from the $1.89 billion reported in June, making July the fourth consecutive month of growth.

Gold financings continued to account for the largest share, despite a slight decline in funds raised. The increase in funds for specialty commodities, particularly rare earth elements, contributed significantly to the rise in July, offsetting declines in base metals financings. The number of transactions in July rose to 317, up from 304 in June. Notably, significant financings — transactions valued at over $2 million — rose to 121 compared to 116 in June. However, there were five transactions valued at over $50 million, a slight decrease from six in June.

July 2025 financing data is available in the accompanying databook.

Gold fell slightly but remains above $1B

Gold financings fell 8% month-over-month to $1.06 billion, following a record high in our data dating back to January 2014. Since the beginning of 2025, gold financings have increased each month except for March, driven by several high-value transactions. The total number of transactions decreased to 149 from 156 in June, while the number of significant transactions fell by one to 61. There were three transactions valued at over $50 million, compared to four in June.

The largest gold financing, and the second-largest overall, was a $218 million follow-on offering by Florida-based Aura Minerals Inc. (AUGO). The proceeds will be used to finance the acquisition of Mineração Serra Grande SA in Brazil from AngloGold Ashanti (AU), as well as the development of the company's Era Dorada and Matupa projects in Guatemala and Brazil, respectively.

Base and other metals group tempered totals

Funds raised for the base and other metals group fell 39% to $371 million in July. All major commodities in this group declined, with silver and copper experiencing the largest drops, at $125 million and $40 million, respectively. Despite the decrease in total funds raised, the number of transactions in this category rose to 104, up from 97 in June, while the number of significant financings increased to 41 from 37. There were no transactions valued at over $50 million, down from two in June.

The largest financing in this category, and the eighth-largest overall, was a C$55 million private placement offering by Vancouver-based Foran Mining Corp. (OTCQX:FMCXF). This transaction is part of the company's plan to raise C$350 million for the construction of the McIlvenna Bay copper mine in Saskatchewan, as well as to advance exploration at near-mine and regional targets.

Lithium fell slightly, but rare earth and tungsten boost specialty group

After declining for two consecutive months in June, the specialty commodities group posted a huge increase in July, up by more than sevenfold to $987 million. Lithium financings fell 13% to $60 million, but the largest boosts were from rare earth elements and tungsten financings. The total number of transactions increased to 64 from 51 in June, and the number of significant financings increased to 19 from 17. There were two transactions valued at over $50 million in July, up from none in June.

The largest transaction overall was a $650 million follow-on offering placement of common stock by Nevada-based MP Materials Corp. (MP). The company plans to allocate the proceeds to fund the acceleration and expansion of operations of the 10X Facility, a magnet manufacturing plant aiming to boost the US production of rare earth magnets. The company owns the Mountain Pass rare earth mine in California and the Independence magnetics facility in Texas.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Absci: Still A Work In Progress, Fighting To Survive
data.description:
Investment Overview

In my last post on Absci (NASDAQ:ABSI) for Seeking Alpha, written in May last year, I was somewhat critical of companies using AI to try to develop new and better medicines, which is the Vancouver based company's core business.

I noted that the company raised ~$230m via its July 2021 IPO, selling 12.5m shares at $16, and that they had subsequently fallen in value to <$1.5, and also noted the disappointing performance of other AI drug discovery companies that had completed similarly large IPO's, writing:

AbCellera Biologics Inc. (ABCL), based in Vancouver like Absci, raised ~$550m at $20 per share in 2020. Schrödinger (SDGR) raised $232m at $17 per share in 2020. Certara, Inc. (CERT) raised ~$335m in 2020 at $23 per share. Recursion Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (RXRX) raised ~$500m in 2021, at $18 per share. Exscientia raised $510m at $22 per share in 2021.

Every company mentioned above bar Certara - down nearly 40% - is down >65% since IPO, with three companies' share prices down >75%. Over the same period, the S&P 500 (SP500) is +20%.

This remains the case today, although Exscientia has since become part of Recursion. In fairness, AbCellera stock is now up >65% on a 12 months basis, but still down nearly 80% since IPO.

I was critical of the thesis that if we build a big enough computer, and put enough data in it, it will figure out how to develop better drugs than are on the market today, noting that no successful drugs to date had been developed using AI.

That is a simplistic view - AI in the right hands can be a powerful tool - but my point that this has generally been a bad sector to be invested in, seems to hold true - Absci stock is down nearly 50% since I issued a Hold recommendation last year, concluding:

While the research seems valid and intriguing, were it not for the "AI" tag, Absci Corporation's value in the marketplace would likely not support a market cap valuation much above $250m, based on my experience researching non-AI based biotech companies and their pipeline.

Presently, Absci's share price is $2.53, and its market cap valuation is $378m. In this note, I'll update on developments at the company since my last note and speculate about the company's prospects for the next 12-15 months.

Absci - Catching Up On Developments, From May Last Year To Q2 2025 Earnings

One positive of holding the stock of an AI driven drug developer is that shares typically spike in reaction to any positive newsflow, as, for example, Absci's share price did in February this year, apparently in response to a press conference at the White House to unveil a $500bn AI initiative, the Stargate Project.

Unfortunately, the positive sentiment does not typically last and Absci's share price, after spiking in value to >$5 per share, was trading <$2.5 per share just a couple of weeks later. In July, shares also briefly spiked as the company reported a ~$50m fundraising completed at $3 per share. The company filed for a $400m mixed securities shelf in August this year also.

Reporting Q2 earnings, Absci recorded revenue of $0.6m, down from $1.3m in the prior year period, R&D expenses of $20.5m, and a net loss of $(30.6m), compared to $(24.8m) in Q2 2024. Cash position was reported as $117.5m

Before we consider Absci's current platform, let's remind ourselves of the company's approach, as per a business description included in the Q2 earnings release:

Our Integrated Drug Creation platform combines cutting-edge AI models with a synthetic biology data engine, enabling the rapid design of innovative therapeutics that address challenging therapeutic targets. Absci’s approach leverages a continuous feedback loop between advanced AI algorithms and wet lab validation. Each cycle refines our data and strengthens our models, facilitating rapid innovation and enhancing the precision of our therapeutic designs.

The below slide taken from a recent investor presentation also highlights some of the work the company has been engaged in, which is intriguing and impressive, in my view, if not yet lucrative in any way.

Now let's consider the pipeline:

As we can see, Absci's pipeline is immature, with only a single asset in a clinical study, ABS-101, indicated for inflammatory bowel disease and targeting the protein TL1A.

Some quick research online tells me that Pharma giants Sanofi (SNY), AbbVie (ABBV), Pfizer (PFE) and Merck (MRK) are all developing drugs that target TL1A, or Tumor Necrosis Factor-like Ligand 1A to give it its full name. Absci believes its drug may have a superior all-round profile, with the potential for once quarterly dosing, and has promised Phase 1 data this year, although it may not reveal much - a Phase 1/2a is slated to begin in early 2026, management says. The market opportunity is >$20bn, Absci believes, but the competition intense.

Meanwhile, ABS-201 targets another large market in androgenetic alopecia, which affects ~80m Americans, Absci says, and has not had a new drug approved since the 1990's. The target is the prolactin receptor ("PRLR"), which drives hair follicle regression, and studies in non-human primates showed that hair density and thickness improved over a short treatment duration. Absci has created a Scientific Advisory Board comprised of hair experts, and it ought to be intriguing to see how that program progresses, with Phase 1/2a studies scheduled for early 2026.

ABS-301 was discovered via Absci's reverse immunology platform, and again, the program looks interesting, with an innovative mechanism of action ("MoA"), as shown in the slide below:

Once again, target markets - a range of solid tumor cancers - run into the double-digit billions, while the final candidate, ABS-501, targets HER2 and is being designed as a more effective trastuzumab - Roche's (OTCQX:RHHBY), which earned ~$1.5bn of revenues last year.

In terms of partnerships, Absci's founder, CEO, President and Director Sean McClain told analyst's on the Q2 earnings call:

earlier this year, AMD - Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) - made a $20 million strategic investment in Absci, reflecting their conviction in the potential of our AI-driven drug creation platform. Our collaboration continues to advance and AMD compute solutions supporting key workloads across our antibody design platform. We will continue to share key updates to this strategic collaboration in the future as they occur.

AMD is a massive company with a market cap of $271bn, and investments of this nature are essentially the lifeblood of companies like Absci, given that the prospect of commercial revenues is still years away.

Absci has attracted staff from companies such as Nvidia (NVDA), Open AI, and Google, and Pharmas including Pfizer, AstraZeneca (AZN), and Novo Nordisk (NVO), and from a research perspective, the Broad Institute, all of which is encouraging in my view. The company has a 77k square foot "drug creation and wet lab space" in Vancouver, and an Innovation Centre in Zug, Switzerland, the headquarters of Crispr Therapeutics and an established biotech hub.

The company says it has been involved in 25-partnered programs to date, including Pharmas Merck and AstraZeneca, and worked on an intriguing project with Caltech focused on HIV, and on data programs with Nvidia and Oracle. A note in the company presentation states that the it anticipates "signing one or more partnerships, including with a Large Pharma in 2025".

Analysis - Intriguing Programs But No Value Proposition - Yet

In all honesty, since my last note I'd say Absci has made incremental, as opposed to rapid progress - there has not been much in the way of share price needle moving events or developments, which explains the company's declining share price.

This is the reality for most, if not all AI-driven drug developers in my view - breakthroughs in biotech are not easy to come by, and when building a drug candidate from scratch, researching the best targets, progress is only ever likely to be slow in the first few years - possibly even the first 10-15 years.

Does that make Absci uninvestable? Not necessarily. Preclinical and Phase 1 data, except in the rarest of circumstances, does not drive share price gains for biotechs, because the market is aware that on only ~10% of occasions does promising early data translate into a successful drug candidate. What's needed is compelling Phase 2 "proof-of-concept" data, and that is 18-24 months away for Absci, perhaps longer, and for a single drug candidate, or perhaps two - ABS-101 and ABS-201.

Absci needs to make sure it can survive financially - the company made a net loss of $(56.7m) in 1H25, and reported current assets of $137.4m, therefore investors should be aware there is a risk of the company running out of funding potentially before the end of next year, although my feeling is there is enough goodwill in the market for the company to be able raise further funding if necessary.

Investors in Absci need to acknowledge that this is a fledgling company, and the tangible risk that despite best efforts, long-term survival is not guaranteed. I'd speculate the company is simply in "survival" mode at this time, attempting to refine its technology and approach, and may need to develop and guide many more assets through the clinical study process before the market gets excited about a potentially commercially viable drug and buys in.

Attracting a "Big Pharma" partner or further investment from an Nvidia or an AMD would be a huge boon for the company, and the only way I see the share price picking up value in the near term - potentially a significant amount of value. Short-term investors may find that prospect a reason for buying stock, but ultimately, when investing in Absci, investors should be thinking about a 10-15 year horizon.

If Absci still exists as a company in 10-15 years time it's possible it may have become a commercial stage Pharma, developing a wide range of assets, and working with numerous partners, within an industry with very high barriers to entry. That is the bull case, the bear case being that the company is unable to attract enough investment in the short-to-medium term, and ends up delisting, or merging with another company, as Exscientia did.

Due to the risks involved, I remain on the side-lines, although having one or two AI-drug developers in your investment portfolio is not necessarily a bad idea, provided you are investing money you can afford to lose, because if this industry does eventually start "winning", it will likely "win big".

data.title: Lazard Equity Franchise Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Global equity markets rose in the second quarter, in a volatile period marked by a steep sell-off after the rollout of US tariffs in early April, which was then followed by a recovery through May and June. US trade policy remained a focal point for investors, as negotiations between the US and its major trading partners continued amid hopes that the US will adopt a more accommodating stance on import taxes. The US stock market ended the quarter at a new record high despite uncertainty about tariffs and the Federal Reserve’s decision to hold interest rates steady because of it. The strong rally staged by European equity markets in the first two months of the quarter fizzled in June, with most major markets posting minor losses in local terms. Inflation numbers picked up in June, fueling speculation that the European Central Bank might pause its rate-cutting campaign.

Portfolio Review

In the second quarter, the Lazard Equity Franchise Portfolio rose in absolute terms but underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI World Index. (Excess return is measured net of fees and in US dollars.)

Contributors

  • Shares in Nexi (OTCPK:NEXXY)(6.4% weighting) traded higher after the Italy-based payments company reported solid first-quarter results, highlighted by strong operational performance, thanks to sustained organic growth and improving margins. We believe that the company's ability to effectively extract synergies from past consolidations and its preparedness to navigate the evolving payment landscape will be pivotal in supporting a further share price rerating.
  • Shares of United Utilities (OTCPK:UUGRY)(3.4% weighting) rose after the UK-based water provider reported strong fiscal-year results that highlighted its robust conclusion to the 2020-2025 regulatory period. Shares received an additional boost from United Utilities’s investor day, which bolstered market confidence that the company had efficiently prepared for the step-up in investment for the 2025-2030 period, and that the targeted 100-basis point outperformance was likely conservative. We view the elevated UK inflation rate of 3.4% as a favorable factor for the company. Looking ahead, we believe that improved returns, combined with the scale of the upcoming investment program, will drive significant earnings growth over the next five years.
  • Traders bid up shares of FDJ United (OTCPK:LFDJF)(5.3% weighting) on bullish sentiment about the financial targets disclosed at the France-based gaming giant’s investor day. With a target of 5% organic growth and a margin for earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) exceeding 26% by 2028, the company’s financial plan is significantly above our forecasts, giving us confidence in the potential upside for the stock.

Detractors

  • Shares of US-based advertising and marketing services provider Omnicom (OMC)(6.0% weighting) traded lower after a sell-side downgrade of the agency industry, driven by macroeconomic risks and concerns that artificial intelligence (AI) could disintermediate advertising agencies. While the potential impact of AI remains uncertain, Omnicom has a strong track record of successfully navigating technological changes in the advertising industry. Over the past 15 years, the company has grown free cash flow per share at an annual rate of 8%, during a period of significant technological disruption. This era saw the rise of the internet, search engines, mobile technology, and social media, which transformed the media and advertising landscape, with digital advertising increasing from 20% to 65% of total ad spending. Historically, clients have tended to reinvest savings generated by technology into additional advertising due to competitive pressures, relying on Omnicom’s expertise to manage the added complexity of fragmented media channels. Omnicom is currently trading at an earnings multiple of 8.3, its lowest stock market valuation in 30 years. Our analysis indicates that the current share price implies a ~30% decline in earnings over the next decade—a scenario we believe is not supported by the available evidence.
  • Despite reporting solid first-quarter results that included 7% organic growth, 200 bps of margin expansion and 14% growth in earning per share, Fiserv (FI)(5.3% weighting), a US-based technology provider of payments and financial services, saw its share price fall on concerns slowing quarter-over-quarter growth in gross payment volumes at Clover, the company’s fast growing point of sale and software product for small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs). Nonetheless, Clover revenues grew 27%, and the company has not changed its medium- or long-run revenue targets for this segment. Our valuation for the business has not materially changed, and we believe the share price fall represents an attractive entry point for long-term investors.
  • Traders sold off shares of Baxter International (BAX)(3.8% weighting) on concerns that the US-based medical device maker will be adversely impacted by US tariffs. As a manufacturer in the US and offshore, the tariffs could in theory impact profit margins. However, Baxter has located its manufacturing facilities in the same countries where it sells its products, potentially minimizing the potential impact from goods subject to US import taxes. The company’s guidance for fiscal year 2025 suggested modest impact from the proposed tariffs.

Outlook

The Equity Franchise Portfolio currently holds market leaders and monopolies with higher long-run earnings predictability than those of the market and that are trading at reasonable valuations. The Portfolio is now trading at a sizable discount to intrinsic value, as well as the broader MSCI World Index on a number of valuation measures. We believe the economic franchise characteristics we seek for all our investments will continue to serve our investors well over the long run.

Important Information

Please consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses carefully before investing. For more complete information about The Lazard Funds, Inc. and current performance, you may obtain a prospectus or summary prospectus by calling 800-823-6300 or going to www.lazardassetmanagement.com. Read the prospectus or summary prospectus carefully before you invest. The prospectus and summary prospectus contain investment objectives, risks, charges, expenses, and other information about the Portfolio and The Lazard Funds that may not be detailed in this document. The Lazard Funds are distributed by Lazard Asset Management Securities LLC.

Information and opinions presented have been obtained or derived from sources believed by Lazard Asset Management LLC or its affiliates (“Lazard”) to be reliable. Lazard makes no representation as to their accuracy or completeness. All opinions expressed herein are as of the published date and are subject to change.

The performance quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. An investor may obtain performance data current to the most recent month-end online at www.lazardassetmanagement.com. The investment return and principal value of the Portfolio will fluctuate; an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost.

Different share classes may have different returns and different investment minimums.

Please click here for standardized returns.

The performance quoted represents past performance. Past performance is not a reliable indicator of future results.

Allocations and security selection are subject to change.

Mention of these securities should not be considered a recommendation or solicitation to purchase or sell the securities. It should not be assumed that any investment in these securities was, or will prove to be, profitable, or that the investment decisions we make in the future will be profitable or equal to the investment performance of securities referenced herein. There is no assurance that any securities referenced herein are currently held in the portfolio or that securities sold have not been repurchased. The securities mentioned may not represent the entire portfolio.

Equity securities will fluctuate in price; the value of your investment will thus fluctuate, and this may result in a loss. Securities in certain non-domestic countries may be less liquid, more volatile, and less subject to governmental supervision than in one’s home market. The values of these securities may be affected by changes in currency rates, application of a country’s specific tax laws, changes in government administration, and economic and monetary policy.

The MSCI World Index is a free-float-adjusted market capitalization index that is designed to measure global developed market equity performance comprised of developed market country indices. The index is unmanaged and has no fees. One cannot invest directly in an index.

Certain information included herein is derived by Lazard in part from an MSCI index or indices (the “Index Data”). However, MSCI has not reviewed this product or report, and does not endorse or express any opinion regarding this product or report or any analysis or other information contained herein or the author or source of any such information or analysis. MSCI makes no express or implied warranties or representations and shall have no liability whatsoever with respect to any Index Data or data derived therefrom.

Certain information contained herein constitutes “forward-looking statements” which can be identified by the use of forward-looking terminology such as “may,” “will,” “should,” “expect,” “anticipate,” “target,” “intent,” “continue,” or “believe,” or the negatives thereof or other variations thereon or comparable terminology. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in such forward-looking statements.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
